title: M.I.T. Computer Program Predicts in 1973 That Civilization Will End by 2040
description: In 1704, Isaac NewôÙton preôÙdictôÙed the end of the world someôÙtime around (or after, ãbut not beforeã) the year 2060, using a strange series o
body:
In 1704, Isaac NewôÙton preôÙdictôÙed the end of the world someôÙtime around (or after, ãbut not beforeã) the year 2060, using a strange series of mathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal calôÙcuôÙlaôÙtions. Rather than study what he called the ãbook of nature,ã he took as his source the supôÙposed propheôÙcies of the Book of RevôÙeôÙlaôÙtion. While such preôÙdicôÙtions have always been cenôÙtral to ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙty, it is starôÙtling for modôÙern peoôÙple to look back and see the famed astronomer and physiôÙcist indulging them. For NewôÙton, howôÙevôÙer, as Matthew StanôÙley writes at SciôÙence, ãlayôÙing the founôÙdaôÙtion of modôÙern physics and astronôÙoôÙmy was a bit of a sideshow. He believed that his truôÙly imporôÙtant work was deciôÙpherôÙing ancient scripôÙtures and uncovôÙerôÙing the nature of the ChrisôÙtôÙian reliôÙgion.ã
Over three hunôÙdred years latôÙer, we still have plenôÙty of reliôÙgious doomôÙsayôÙers preôÙdictôÙing the end of the world with Bible codes. But in recent times, their ranks have seemôÙingôÙly been joined by sciôÙenôÙtists whose only proôÙfessed aim is interôÙpretôÙing data from cliôÙmate research and susôÙtainôÙabilôÙiôÙty estiôÙmates givôÙen popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion growth and dwinôÙdling resources. The sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic preôÙdicôÙtions do not draw on ancient texts or theôÙolôÙoôÙgy, nor involve final batôÙtles between good and evil. Though there may be plagues and othôÙer horôÙriôÙble reckôÙonôÙings, these are preôÙdictably causal outôÙcomes of over-proôÙducôÙtion and conôÙsumpôÙtion rather than divine wrath. Yet by some strange fluke, the sciôÙence has arrived at the same apocôÙaôÙlypôÙtic date as NewôÙton, plus or minus a decade or two.
The ãend of the worldã in these sceôÙnarôÙios means the end of modôÙern life as we know it: the colôÙlapse of indusôÙtriôÙalôÙized sociôÙeties, large-scale agriôÙculôÙturôÙal proôÙducôÙtion, supôÙply chains, staôÙble cliôÙmates, nation statesãÎ. Since the late sixôÙties, an elite sociôÙety of wealthy indusôÙtriôÙalôÙists and sciôÙenôÙtists known as the Club of Rome (a freôÙquent playôÙer in many conôÙspirôÙaôÙcy theôÙoôÙries) has foreôÙseen these disôÙasôÙters in the earôÙly 21st cenôÙtuôÙry. One of the sources of their vision is a comôÙputôÙer proôÙgram develôÙoped at MIT by comôÙputôÙing pioôÙneer and sysôÙtems theôÙoôÙrist Jay ForôÙrester, whose modôÙel of globôÙal susôÙtainôÙabilôÙiôÙty, one of the first of its kind, preôÙdictôÙed civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtionôÙal colôÙlapse in 2040. ãWhat the comôÙputôÙer enviôÙsioned in the 1970s has by and large been comôÙing true,ã claims Paul RatôÙner at Big Think.
Those preôÙdicôÙtions include popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion growth and polôÙluôÙtion levôÙels, ãworsôÙenôÙing qualôÙiôÙty of life,ã and ãdwinôÙdling natôÙurôÙal resources.ã In the video at the top, see AusôÙtraliôÙaãs ABC explain the computerãs calôÙcuôÙlaôÙtions, ãan elecôÙtronôÙic guidôÙed tour of our globôÙal behavôÙior since 1900, and where that behavôÙior will lead us,ã says the preôÙsenôÙter. The graph spans the years 1900 to 2060. ãQualôÙiôÙty of lifeã begins to sharply decline after 1940, and by 2020, the modôÙel preôÙdicts, the metôÙric conôÙtracts to turn-of-the-cenôÙtuôÙry levôÙels, meetôÙing the sharp increase of the ãZed Curveã that charts polôÙluôÙtion levôÙels. (ABC revisôÙitôÙed this reportôÙing in 1999 with Club of Rome memôÙber KeiôÙth Suter.)
You can probôÙaôÙbly guess the restãor you can read all about it in the 1972 Club of Rome-pubôÙlished report LimôÙits to Growth, which drew wide popôÙuôÙlar attenôÙtion to Jay Forresterãs books Urban DynamôÙics (1969) and World DynamôÙics (1971). ForôÙrester, a figôÙure of NewôÙtonôÙian stature in the worlds of comôÙputôÙer sciôÙence and manôÙageôÙment and sysôÙtems theoryãthough not, like NewôÙton, a BibôÙliôÙcal propheôÙcy enthusiastãmore or less endorsed his conôÙcluôÙsions to the end of his life in 2016. In one of his last interôÙviews, at the age of 98, he told the MIT TechôÙnolôÙoôÙgy Review, ãI think the books stand all right.ã But he also cauôÙtioned against actôÙing withôÙout sysôÙtemôÙatôÙic thinkôÙing in the face of the globôÙalôÙly interôÙreôÙlatôÙed issues the Club of Rome omiôÙnousôÙly calls ãthe probôÙlemôÙatôÙicã:
Time after time ãÎ youãll find peoôÙple are reactôÙing to a probôÙlem, they think they know what to do, and they donãt realôÙize that what theyãre doing is makôÙing a probôÙlem. This is a vicious [cycle], because as things get worse, there is more incenôÙtive to do things, and it gets worse and worse.
Where this vague warnôÙing is supôÙposed to leave us is uncerôÙtain. If the curôÙrent course is dire, ãunsysôÙtemôÙatôÙicã soluôÙtions may be worse? This theôÙoôÙry also seems to leave powôÙerôÙfulôÙly vestôÙed human agents (like Exxonãs execôÙuôÙtives) wholôÙly unacôÙcountôÙable for the comôÙing colôÙlapse. LimôÙits to Growthãscoffed at and disôÙparagôÙingôÙly called ãneo-MalthuôÙsianã by a host of libôÙerôÙtarôÙiôÙan critôÙicsãstands on far surôÙer eviôÙdenôÙtiary footôÙing than Newtonãs weird preôÙdicôÙtions, and its cliôÙmate foreôÙcasts, notes ChrisôÙtôÙian ParôÙenôÙti, ãwere alarmôÙingôÙly preôÙscient.ã But for all this doom and gloom itãs worth bearôÙing in mind that modôÙels of the future are not, in fact, the future. There are hard times ahead, but no theôÙoôÙry, no matôÙter how sophisôÙtiôÙcatôÙed, can account for every variôÙable.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
In 1953, a TeleôÙphone-ComôÙpaôÙny ExecôÙuôÙtive PreôÙdicts the Rise of ModôÙern SmartôÙphones and Video Calls
In 1922, a NovôÙelôÙist PreôÙdicts What the World Will Look Like in 2022: WireôÙless TeleôÙphones, 8ãHour Flights to Europe & More
In 1704, Isaac NewôÙton PreôÙdicts the World Will End in 2060
Itãs the End of the World as We Know It: The ApocôÙaôÙlypse Gets VisuôÙalôÙized in an InvenôÙtive Map from 1486
Watch the DestrucôÙtion of PomôÙpeii by Mount VesuôÙvius, Re-CreôÙatôÙed with ComôÙputôÙer AniôÙmaôÙtion (79 AD)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: The Unexpected Math Behind Van Goghãs ãStarry Nightã
description:
in Art, Math |   	May 28th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

If youãve takôÙen a good art hisôÙtoôÙry course on the ImpresôÙsionôÙists and Post-ImpresôÙsionôÙists
body:
in Art, Math |   	May 28th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

If youãve takôÙen a good art hisôÙtoôÙry course on the ImpresôÙsionôÙists and Post-ImpresôÙsionôÙists, youãve inevitably encounôÙtered VinôÙcent van Goghãs 1889 masôÙterôÙpiece ãStarôÙry Night,ã which now hangs in the MoMA in New York City. The paintôÙing, the museôÙum writes on its webôÙsite, ãis a symôÙbolôÙic landôÙscape full of moveôÙment, enerôÙgy, and light. The quietôÙness of the vilôÙlage conôÙtrasts with the swirling enerôÙgy of the sky.ãÎ Van Goghãs impasôÙto techôÙnique, or thickôÙly applied colôÙors, creôÙates a rhythôÙmic effectãthe picôÙture seems to conôÙstantôÙly move in its frame.ã ArtisôÙtiôÙcalôÙly, van Gogh manôÙaged to capôÙture moveôÙment in a way that no artist had ever quite done it before. SciôÙenôÙtifôÙiôÙcalôÙly, it turns out, he was on to someôÙthing too. Just watch the new TED-ED lesôÙson above, The UnexôÙpectôÙed Math Behind Van Goghãs ãStarôÙry Night.ã
CreôÙatôÙed by math artist/teacher Natalya St. Clair and aniôÙmaôÙtor Avi Ofer, the video explores how ãVan Gogh capôÙtured [the] deep mysôÙtery of moveôÙment, fluôÙid and light in his work,ã and parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly manôÙaged to depict the eluôÙsive pheôÙnomôÙeôÙnon known as turôÙbuôÙlence. In StarôÙry Night, the video observes, van Gogh depictôÙed turôÙbuôÙlence with a degree of sophisôÙtiôÙcaôÙtion and accuôÙraôÙcy that rivals the way physiôÙcists and mathôÙeôÙmatiôÙcians have best explained turôÙbuôÙlence in their own sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic papers. And, it all hapôÙpened, perôÙhaps by coinôÙciôÙdence (?), durôÙing the turôÙbuôÙlent last years of van Goghãs life.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtentô

title: Take a Random Walk Around the Berlin Wall Just Months Before Its Sudden Fall (Summer 1989)
description:
in History, Travel |   	May 27th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

OffiôÙcialôÙly, the Berlin Wall fell on NovemôÙber 9, 1989. DemoôÙliôÙtion would take m
body:
in History, Travel |   	May 27th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

OffiôÙcialôÙly, the Berlin Wall fell on NovemôÙber 9, 1989. DemoôÙliôÙtion would take more than four years, and a few secôÙtions remain for memoôÙrôÙiôÙal purôÙposôÙes, but it was on that date that pasôÙsage between East and West Berlin ã and thus East and West GerôÙmany ã opened to all citôÙiôÙzens of both counôÙtries. To say that it came as a surôÙprise would be a seriôÙous underôÙstateôÙment. EarôÙliôÙer that year, even the best informed observers were preôÙdictôÙing that the wall would stand for at least a few more decades. EarôÙliôÙer that day, for that matôÙter, the offiôÙcials involved in the openôÙing didôÙnãt foreôÙsee that SocialôÙist UniôÙty ParôÙty of GerôÙmany SecôÙreôÙtary of InforôÙmaôÙtion Gû¥nôÙter SchôÙabowsôÙki would, that evening, misôÙtakôÙenôÙly declare on nationôÙal teleôÙviôÙsion that the libôÙerôÙalôÙizaôÙtion of borôÙder travôÙel was effecôÙtive ãimmeôÙdiôÙateôÙly, withôÙout delay.ã
When the borôÙder guards finalôÙly gave up their attempts to hold the line around 11:00 that night, the surôÙroundôÙing scene in both Berlins had turned into what attenôÙdees now rememôÙber, 36 years latôÙer, as the biggest street fesôÙtiôÙval of their lives. To those of us unable to join in the celôÙeôÙbraôÙtion at the time, it may seem unlikeôÙly that such an event could realôÙly have occurred with no intiôÙmaôÙtions whatôÙsoôÙevôÙer.
Yet the footage shot by a travôÙelôÙer in Berlin durôÙing the sumôÙmer of 1989, right there in the vicinôÙiôÙty of the wall, depicts a city where events seem to be frozen. Though the built enviôÙronôÙment isnãt withôÙout touchôÙes of fadôÙed grandeur here and there (and as many West BerlinôÙers were soon to disôÙcovôÙer, the real urban stateôÙliôÙness was over East), the overôÙall impresôÙsion givôÙen by what was then the red hot cenôÙter of Cold War geopolôÙiôÙtics is that of a dullsville.
The most outôÙwardôÙly interôÙestôÙing feaôÙture in these parts of Berlin at the very end of the nineôÙteen-eightôÙies is, of course, the wall itself: the brutishôÙness of its form, the humôÙdrum menôÙace of its guards, the accuôÙmuôÙlaôÙtion of grafôÙfiôÙti both politôÙiôÙcal and apoôÙlitôÙiôÙcal. At one point, the touristãs camôÙcorder capôÙtures the memoôÙriôÙals for fallôÙen wall jumpers, the most recent of which, a cerôÙtain Chris GuefôÙfroy, had made his fateôÙful escape attempt from the East that past FebôÙruôÙary. HisôÙtoôÙry would soon immorôÙtalôÙize him as the last perôÙson to be shot tryôÙing to get over the wall, though not the last to die doing so. That title belongs to WinôÙfried FreudenôÙberg, who in March of 1989 fell from a balôÙloon heãd rigged up to fly across the borôÙder. At this point, when the rapid urban evoôÙluôÙtion of the reuniôÙfied GerôÙman capôÙiôÙtal has long since made it one of the most popôÙuôÙlar cities in Europe, neiôÙther she nor GuefôÙfroy would recôÙogôÙnize the forôÙmer East Berlin they were desôÙperôÙate to escape ã nor, for that matôÙter, the West Berlin of which they dreamed.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

See Berlin Before and After World War II in StarôÙtling ColôÙor Video
The GoldôÙen Age of Berlin Comes to Life in the ClasôÙsic, Avant-Garde Film, Berlin: SymôÙphoôÙny of a MetropôÙoôÙlis (1927)
The Dos & Donãts of DriôÙving to West Berlin DurôÙing the Cold War: A Weird Piece of Ephemera from the 1980s
Bruce SpringôÙsteen Plays East Berlin in 1988: Iãm Not Here For Any GovôÙernôÙment. Iãve Come to Play Rock
Watch Samuel BeckôÙett Walk the Streets of Berlin Like a Boss, 1969
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: A Tour of Athensã Acropolis, Explained with 3D Reconstructions
description:
in Architecture, History |   	May 28th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Since it was first built as a MyceôÙnaean fortress in the thirôÙteenth cenôÙtuôÙ
body:
in Architecture, History |   	May 28th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Since it was first built as a MyceôÙnaean fortress in the thirôÙteenth cenôÙtuôÙry BC, what we now know as the AcropôÙoôÙlis has been used to worôÙship not just Greek gods, but also, in latôÙer periôÙods, the VirôÙgin Mary and Allah. Now, of course, with its days of milôÙiôÙtary and reliôÙgious funcôÙtions long behind it, it stands as a set of ruins. Still, theyãre very popôÙuôÙlar ruins, as eviôÙdenced by the crowds capôÙtured in the video above from Manuel BraôÙvo. Though most tourists at the AcropôÙoôÙlis come with the idea that its buildôÙings would have looked more gloôÙriôÙous in the disôÙtant past, few can have much of a sense of how to imagôÙine that with any accuôÙraôÙcy. Using 3D modôÙels, BraôÙvo inteôÙgrates views of how the Parthenon, the TemôÙple of Athena Nike, and othôÙer strucôÙtures look now with how they would have looked in Athensã goldôÙen age.
To fulôÙly appreôÙciôÙate the AcropôÙoôÙlis requires not just an idea of how it was origôÙiôÙnalôÙly intendôÙed to look, as BraôÙvo emphaôÙsizes, but also the intenôÙtions of ancient Greek archiôÙtecôÙture. The approach up the hill was meant to feel like an ascent from the munôÙdane world into the sacred one.
EnterôÙing the cenôÙtral space on top, the visôÙiôÙtor was led to viewôÙing points that showed the surôÙroundôÙing colôÙlecôÙtion of buildôÙings at their most draôÙmatôÙic, a design the archiôÙtects might have described as cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic, had cinôÙeôÙma existôÙed at the time. Even in its ruined state, the AcropôÙoôÙlis still transôÙmits a sense of how, where, and to what degree that visôÙiôÙtor was meant to be filled with awe, as well as where he was meant to look. And nothôÙing up there ã at least in the absence of Phidiasã thirôÙty-foot statôÙue Athena ProôÙmaôÙchos ã draws attenôÙtion as delibôÙerôÙateôÙly as the Parthenon.
As we preôÙviôÙousôÙly notôÙed here on Open CulôÙture, if you make the trip to the AcropôÙoôÙlis yourôÙself, you can now seeô the Parthenon withôÙout scafôÙfoldôÙing (or, dependôÙing on when you go, a minôÙiôÙmum of scafôÙfoldôÙing) for the first time in 200 years. That lack of obstrucôÙtion makes it easôÙiôÙer to enviôÙsion the gloôÙries of that celôÙeôÙbratôÙed buildôÙing back when it was both the temôÙple of Athena and the treaôÙsury of Athens. But as BraôÙvo says, if you realôÙly want to gaze upon the Parthenon as the ancients knew it, marôÙbles and all, youãll have to make the trek out to Nashville, TenôÙnessee,ô where a full-scale repliôÙca was built in 1897 for the cityãs CenôÙtenôÙniôÙal ExpoôÙsiôÙtion. It may feel a bit odd to turn up in a place known for counôÙtry music and bachôÙeôÙlorette parôÙties in search of the archiôÙtecôÙturôÙal, and perôÙhaps spirôÙiôÙtuôÙal founôÙdaôÙtion of Europe. But then, civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion has nevôÙer takôÙen a preôÙdictable course.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How the Ancient Greeks Built Their MagôÙnifôÙiôÙcent TemôÙples: The Art of Ancient EngiôÙneerôÙing
A 3D ModôÙel Reveals What the Parthenon and Its InteôÙriôÙor Looked Like 2,500 Years Ago
A VirôÙtuôÙal Tour of Ancient Athens: Fly Over ClasôÙsiôÙcal Greek CivôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion in All Its GloôÙry
The City of Nashville Built a Full-Scale RepliôÙca of the Parthenon in 1897, and Itãs Still StandôÙing Today
How the Worldãs Biggest Dome Was Built: The StoôÙry of FilôÙipôÙpo Brunelleschi and the DuoôÙmo in FloôÙrence
Take a High Def, GuidôÙed Tour of PomôÙpeii
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Matte Marble Dust Glimmers Across Marin Majic's Enigmatic Scenes
description: Layers of colored pencil and marble dust worked into an oil-like substance flood the linen planes on which Marin Majic works. The Brooklyn-based a
body:
Layers of colored pencil and marble dust worked into an oil-like substance flood the linen planes on which Marin Majic works. The Brooklyn-based artist builds upon a foundational drawing, blending various media into a richly textured surface resembling fabric or plaster. Matte finishes radiate across the scenes, appearing like magical glimmers under a night sky.
Steeped in mystery, Majicãs works gravitate toward questions of power, impermanence, and the slippery nature of reality. Figures are often alone, whether swimming solo or driving along a mountain pass with no other cars in sight. Insects and animals are similar, although in pieces like ãNegative attention,ã weãre witness to the demise of the others, as a glinting spiderweb clings to a handful of colorful specimens. 
ãNegative attentionã (2026), colored pencil, oil color, and marble dust on linen, 13 x 16 inches. Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein
Deep shadows and haze blanket the scenes, with small pockets of light offering only bits of insight into the full scope of the landscape. The enigmatic nature of Majicãs work also echoes that of his material choices: he utilizes the chalky marble dust to accentuate and define light, creating a twinkling effect with otherwise matte stone.
Majic is currently preparing for several group shows this summer, along with a book release with Nino Mier Gallery. Explore more of his work on Instagram.
Photo by Cary Whittier
ãWhile We Drive.ã Photo by New Document
ãUnder the Radar.ã Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein
ãNight Swim II.ã Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein
Detail of ãNight Swim II.ã Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein
Detail of ãSee You.ã Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein

title: 'BlowUp Jubilee' Celebrates Inflatable Public Art in the Center of The Hague
description: While the Dutch city of The Hague may be best known for the judicial arm of the United Nations, thereãs a lot more to it than global peace and jus
body:
While the Dutch city of The Hague may be best known for the judicial arm of the United Nations, thereãs a lot more to it than global peace and justice organizations. Itãs home to some of Europeãs most esteemed art museums, such as the Mauritshuis, where you can visit Johannes Vermeerãs Girl with a Pearl Earring. Then thereãs the immanently popular Madurodam, a sprawling scale model of some of The Netherlandsã most famous and historic palaces and public buildings. That, in turn, is located in the charming Scheveningen district, which boasts a wide, popular beach on the North Sea.
Madurodam, for one, is where youãll find a handful of playful inflatable art installations as part of BlowUp Jubilee, an anniversary edition of The Hagueãs popular BlowUp Art event. Now in its sixth year, the open-air public art exhibition curated by Mary Hessing brings together 20 pieces that interact with monuments and buildings in a celebration of heritage and access for all.
Eugenie Boon
For BlowUp Jubilee, artists whose work has been shown during the past five years have been invited back. In many cases, the sculptures are in new locations or configurations. Scale, color, and form interact in surprising ways as inflatable orbs, shell-like shapes, spikes, and more reimagine a variety of landmarks.
At Madurodam, size and scale are on particularly curious display, as what appears to be a giant pink chair in the middle of a fountain is actually quite small. Other works around town, however, celebrate historic architecture with genuinely monumental creations, such as Steve Messamãs bulbous, green passageway installation or Eugenie Boonãs ode to her Caribbean heritage in a piece titled ãkoncha pa dilanti,ã which refers to a board game played on the islands. Youãll also find a giant yellow donut by John KûÑrmeling, a pink pavilion by Sigrid Calon, and a cosmic cafe concept by Yamuna Forzani. 
BlowUp Jubilee continues through June 21. Learn more and plan your visit by checking out the eventãs website. You might also enjoy Bubbletecture.
An installation at Madurodam
Steve Messam
Sigrid Calon
Studio Meike Meijer

Foreground: Raw Color
A miniature installation by Steve Messam at Madurodam

title: A Leonora Carrington Biopic Traces the Surrealist Icon's Life and Work
description: ãDonãt you think itãs dangerous to blur the distinction between abstraction and reality?ã asks actress Olivia Vinall in her role as the Surrealist
body:
ãDonãt you think itãs dangerous to blur the distinction between abstraction and reality?ã asks actress Olivia Vinall in her role as the Surrealist artist and writer Leonora Carringtonô (1917-2011). The heady line is one of the standout statements in the new biopic documenting Carringtonãs life and work.
Directed by Thor Klein and Lena Vurma and produced by Modern Films, Leonora in the Morning Light opens in 1930s Paris, when the artist was enmeshed in an avant-garde community that included luminaries like Salvador DalûÙ and Andrûˋ Breton, along with her partner Max Ernst. When World War II begins, Carrington flees to Spain before eventually re-settling in Mexico, perhaps the location most associated with her work.

The film is based on the biographical novel by Elena Poniatowska and comes at a time when Carringtonãs oeuvre is in the spotlight, particularly the fantastical work made while she was confined to a psychiatric hospital in Spain which had been lost for 80 years.
Modern Films is also behind the documentary about pioneering artist Hilma af Klint, along with Boom For Real, which chronicles Jean-Michel Basquiatãs teenage years. Leonora in the Morning Light is slated for release on May 29 in the U.K. and Ireland.
